source.title: The Predictive Coding Account of Psychosis

confidence_rationale: Comprehensive review co-authored by the leading figures in the predictive-coding-of-psychosis literature (Adams, Fletcher, Frith, Corlett). Explicitly states that 'canonical theories do not highlight active inference -- the process through which the effects of our actions on our sensations are anticipated and minimized.' Also explicitly notes that the persistence of delusions (as distinct from their emergence) 'might result from decreased precision of priors as outlined above' for emergence but requires 'an excessive influence of delusional beliefs' for persistence -- a tension the canonical account does not resolve. Source quality maximal; mapping fidelity high for the orthogonality question (this is the paper that establishes the gap the proposed mechanism would fill).
failure_signatures: The review does not endorse the commit-boundary belief lock framing -- it acknowledges the gap (active inference / action-loop missing from canonical accounts; persistence vs emergence asymmetry; ketamine / memory-reconsolidation evidence linking PE to belief strengthening) but offers different directions for filling it (hierarchical predictive coding, social priors, beliefs-as-utility). The proposal must show that its framing adds explanatory work beyond what these alternatives can do.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Canonical predictive-coding accounts of psychosis (decreased precision of priors relative to sensory data) face several unresolved challenges, including: (1) some studies implicate weakened priors and others stronger priors; (2) hallucinations and delusions may relate to distinct alterations despite their co-occurrence; (3) canonical theories do not address active inference (the action-loop); (4) the emergence vs persistence asymmetry of delusions is unexplained -- emergence may be weak-prior, but persistence requires excessive belief influence on perception, with ketamine/memory-reconsolidation evidence linking prediction error signaling to delusional belief strengthening.
mapping.ree_translation: Direct support for the orthogonality argument that the brief requires. The proposed commit-boundary belief lock mechanism would fill three of the gaps Sterzer et al. flag: (a) the missing active-inference / action-loop component; (b) the emergence-vs-persistence asymmetry (commit-boundary lock is specifically a persistence mechanism); (c) the role of memory and reconsolidation in belief strengthening. The proposed mechanism does not claim to replace the canonical account; it claims to supplement it with the action-loop component the canonical account explicitly admits to lacking.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Sterzer et al. do not endorse the commit-boundary framing or anything close to it -- they propose hierarchical predictive coding, social priors, and beliefs-as-utility as their preferred candidates for the gaps they flag. The proposed mechanism must therefore demonstrate explanatory work beyond what these alternatives can do, particularly for the action-history asymmetry the brief targets. The orthogonality argument is supported; the registration argument requires additional discriminative work.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review; ten-author panel from the leading European/UK predictive-coding-of-psychosis labs; published in Biological Psychiatry.

confidence_components.notes: Biological Psychiatry; the review is the canonical reference for the state of predictive-coding accounts of psychosis. Mapping is direct for the orthogonality question (the paper itself flags the gaps). Transfer risk is low because the source domain (theoretical psychiatry) is the same as the target domain (mechanistic theory of delusion).
